attributes.stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic wanting to lose weight. He has a medical history of hypertension and mild asthma. His regular medications are perindopril and a salbutamol inhaler as needed. Over the last six months, he has implemented a regular exercise schedule and followed dietary advice from a dietician, resulting in a 5 kg weight loss. However, he feels his weight loss has stagnated. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His BMI is 33 kg/m2. He is clear that he does not want to consider surgical intervention.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate pharmaceutical intervention?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is a candidate for pharmaceutical intervention for obesity, having a BMI >30 kg/m2 and not achieving more than 5% total body weight loss in six months despite lifestyle modifications. The choice of pharmaceutical option should be individualised based on the patient's comorbidities, potential drug interactions, contraindications, and adverse effects.

Liraglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist initially used to treat type 2 diabetes mellitus. It is now also indicated for weight management. While it is expensive and requires subcutaneous administration, it has proven efficacy in weight reduction and a more acceptable side effect profile compared to other options. These factors make it the preferred choice for many clinicians and patients.

Cagrilintide is another GLP-1 receptor agonist that has shown promise in clinical trials for weight management. However, it is not currently available on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme (PBS) in Australia, making it less accessible and more expensive than liraglutide.

Orlistat works by inhibiting intestinal lipases, decreasing fat absorption. While it is a cheaper option, it has fallen out of favour due to its side effect profile, which includes fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, steatorrhoea, oily spotting, faecal incontinence, and flatulence. It is also less efficacious than liraglutide for weight loss.

Phentermine is a sympathomimetic agent used for short-term appetite suppression. It has a slight potential for abuse and should not be used long-term due to adverse cardiovascular effects, including raised blood pressure. Given this patient's history of hypertension and the likely need for long-term treatment, phentermine is not the best option.

Naltrexone-bupropion is a combination therapy containing an opioid receptor antagonist (naltrexone) and a noradrenaline-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (bupropion). While it can be effective for weight loss, it is not recommended as a first-line treatment due to its cardiovascular adverse effects (including hypertension) and high side effect profile, which includes nightmares, agitation, seizures, and hypersensitivity reactions.

In summary, liraglutide offers the best balance of efficacy and tolerability for this patient, making it the most appropriate pharmaceutical intervention for obesity management in this case.
learningPoints: GLP-1 receptor agonists are currently the preferred pharmaceutical agents in the management of obesity due to their efficacy and acceptable side effect profile.
Pharmaceutical intervention for obesity is appropriate for patients with a BMI >30 kg/m2 who have not achieved >5% total body weight loss in six months with lifestyle modifications.
references: Proietto J. Medicines for long-term obesity management. Aust Prescr 2022;45:38-40. Available at: https://doi.org/10.18773/austprescr.2022.009

Shi Q, Wang Y, Has, QH, et al. Pharmacotherapy for adults with overweight and obesity: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials. Lancet Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(21)01640-8
Lee PC, Dixon J. Pharmacotherapy for obesity. Australian family physician. 2017 Jul;46(7):472-7. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2017/july/pharmacotherapy-for-obesity
